column1: Vairocana is regarded as the cosmic Buddha, embodiment of the Dharmakāya, whose light pervades the universe and represents the ultimate truth and wisdom158.
The five-colored light symbolizes the five Buddha families, each associated with a direction, color, and aspect of enlightenment710.
Amitābha means “Immeasurable Light”; his Pure Land is described as pervaded by infinite, radiant light that liberates beings from suffering6911.
The 48 vows, especially the vow of infinite light, are central to Pure Land faith and practice.
Lamp imagery in Dzogchen and Vajrayana refers to the direct recognition of mind’s luminous nature and the dissolution into the dharmakāya.
Solar Light (Vairocana): Absolute truth, cosmic wisdom, universal illumination15.
Lunar Light (Amitābha): Compassion, meditative calm, salvific means69.
Rainbow Light (Dzogchen): Mind’s natural state, realization of primordial awareness.

Vairocana and Amitābha represent two archetypal light systems in Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism. Vairocana’s light is cosmic, all-pervading, and symbolizes the ultimate reality and wisdom, while Amitābha’s light is boundless, compassionate, and salvific, drawing beings to the Pure Land. Esoteric lamp and chakra systems further elaborate the symbolism of inner illumination and realization across Buddhist traditions1567910.
